```{python}
    Pareto chart withfull dynamic scaling, label wrapping, and optional horizontal orientation.
    horizontal=False  -> standard vertical bars
    horizontal=True   -> flipped bars (categories on y-axis)
    """
    # Count and limit to top N
    vc = df[column].value_counts()
    actual_n = min(top_n, len(vc))
    vc = vc.head(actual_n)
    cum_pct = vc.cumsum() / vc.sum()

    # Wrap long labels for readability
    wrapped_labels = [textwrap.fill(str(lbl), width=18) for lbl in vc.index]

    # Dynamic figure size
    if horizontal:
        fig_width = 14
        fig_height = max(6, actual_n * 0.45)
    else:
        fig_width = max(12, actual_n * 0.6)
        fig_height = 6

    fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(fig_width, fig_height))

    # Horizontal orientation
    if horizontal:
        sns.barplot(y=wrapped_labels, x=vc.values, ax=ax1, color="skyblue")
        ax1.set_xlabel("Count")
        ax1.set_ylabel(column)

        # Cumulative line
        ax2 = ax1.twiny()
        ax2.plot(cum_pct.values, wrapped_labels, color="red", marker="o")
        ax2.set_xlabel("Cumulative %")

        plt.title(f"Pareto Chart — Top {actual_n} {column} (horizontal)")

    # Vertical orientation
    else:
        sns.barplot(x=wrapped_labels, y=vc.values, ax=ax1, color="skyblue")
        ax1.set_ylabel("Count")
        ax1.set_xlabel(column)
        plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")

        # Cumulative line
        ax2 = ax1.twinx()
        ax2.plot(wrapped_labels, cum_pct.values, color="red", marker="o")
        ax2.set_ylabel("Cumulative %")

        plt.title(f"Pareto Chart — Top {actual_n} {column}")

    # Improve spacing
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Time Series Plot (Data-aware, safe)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_time_series(
    df,
    date_col,
    value_col,
    freq=None,
    title=None,
    y_label=None,
    rolling=None,              # e.g., [7, 30]
    compare_with=None,         # e.g., "net_sales_amount"
    compare_label=None,
    compare_color="darkred"
):
    """
    Individual time-series plot with:
    - Automatic semantic labeling (numeric vs non-numeric)
    - Optional manual overrides for title and y-axis label
    - Optional rolling averages (7-day, 30-day, etc.)
    - Optional dual-axis overlay for comparing two metrics
    """

    # Validate columns
    if date_col not in df.columns:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Date column '{date_col}' not found.")
        return

    if value_col not in df.columns:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Value column '{value_col}' not found.")
        return

    df_local = df.copy()
    df_local[date_col] = pd.to_datetime(df_local[date_col])

    # Detect numeric vs non-numeric
    is_numeric = pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df_local[value_col])

    # Auto-convert non-numeric to count
    if not is_numeric:
        df_local["_value"] = 1
        auto_metric_label = f"Count of {value_col}"
        value_col = "_value"
    else:
        auto_metric_label = value_col

    # Manual override for y-axis label
    metric_label = y_label if y_label is not None else auto_metric_label

    # Infer frequency
    if freq is None:
        freq = _infer_freq_from_dates(df_local, date_col)

    # Resample
    ts = df_local.set_index(date_col)[value_col].resample(freq).sum()

    if ts.dropna().empty:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] No data available after resampling {date_col} at '{freq}'.")
        return

    # Auto title
    auto_title = f"{metric_label} Over Time (freq='{freq}')"
    final_title = title if title is not None else auto_title

    # Plot base series
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
    ts.plot(ax=ax, color="steelblue", label=metric_label)

    # Rolling averages
    if rolling:
        for window in rolling:
            if window > 1:
                ts.rolling(window).mean().plot(
                    ax=ax,
                    linewidth=2,
                    label=f"{window}-period MA"
                )

    # Dual-axis overlay
    if compare_with:
        if compare_with not in df.columns:
            print(f"[SKIPPED] Compare column '{compare_with}' not found.")
        else:
            # Detect numeric vs non-numeric for comparison metric
            is_num2 = pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df[compare_with])
            if not is_num2:
                df_local["_cmp"] = 1
                cmp_col = "_cmp"
                cmp_label = f"Count of {compare_with}"
            else:
                cmp_col = compare_with
                cmp_label = compare_with

            cmp_series = df_local.set_index(date_col)[cmp_col].resample(freq).sum()

            ax2 = ax.twinx()
            ax2.plot(
                cmp_series.index,
                cmp_series.values,
                color=compare_color,
                alpha=0.7,
                label=compare_label or cmp_label
            )
            ax2.set_ylabel(compare_label or cmp_label)

    ax.set_title(final_title)
    ax.set_ylabel(metric_label)
    ax.set_xlabel("Date")
    ax.legend(loc="upper left")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Heatmap (Hour × Day or Month × Year)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_heatmap(df, row_col, col_col):
    pivot = df.pivot_table(
        index=row_col,
        columns=col_col,
        aggfunc="size",
        fill_value=0
    )

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap="Blues")

    plt.title(f"Heatmap: {row_col} × {col_col}")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 7. Scatter Plot (Relationships)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_scatter(df, x, y, hue=None, log_x=False, log_y=False):
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
    sns.scatterplot(data=df, x=x, y=y, hue=hue, alpha=0.6)

    if log_x:
        plt.xscale("log")
    if log_y:
        plt.yscale("log")

    plt.title(f"{x} vs {y}")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 8. Correlation Heatmap
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_corr(df, title="Correlation Heatmap"):
    corr = df.corr(numeric_only=True)

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
    sns.heatmap(corr, cmap="coolwarm", center=0, annot=False)

    plt.title(title)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 9. Funnel Chart (Simple, Data-aware)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_funnel(stages, values):
    if len(stages) != len(values):
        print("[SKIPPED] Stages and values must have the same length.")
        return

    s = pd.Series(values, index=stages)
    s = s[s > 0]

    if s.empty:
        print("[SKIPPED] No positive values available for funnel chart.")
        return

    actual_n = len(s)

    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
    sns.barplot(x=s.values, y=s.index, palette="viridis")

    plt.title(f"Funnel Chart — {actual_n} Stages")
    plt.xlabel("Count")
    plt.ylabel("Stage")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 10. Anomaly Scatter (Isolation Forest, Data-aware)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_anomaly(df, x, y, contamination=0.02):
    """
    Scatter‑plot anomaly detection using IsolationForest.

    Improvements added:
    --------------------
    1. Human‑readable labels:
       - IsolationForest outputs {1: normal, -1: anomaly}
       - These are mapped to {"Normal", "Anomaly"} for clearer interpretation.

    2. Safer dtype validation:
       Ensures both x and y are numeric before fitting the model.

    3. Cleaned plotting layer:
       Uses a categorical hue with explicit colors for Normal vs Anomaly.

    Parameters:
    -----------
    df : DataFrame
        Input dataset.
    x, y : str
        Numeric columns to use for anomaly detection.
    contamination : float
        Expected proportion of anomalies in the dataset.
        Passed directly to IsolationForest.
    """

    # Validate columns
    if x not in df.columns or y not in df.columns:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Columns '{x}' or '{y}' not found.")
        return

    # Ensure numeric inputs
    if not pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df[x]) or not pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df[y]):
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Columns '{x}' and '{y}' must be numeric.")
        return

    # Drop missing values
    clean_df = df[[x, y]].dropna()

    if clean_df.shape[0] < 10:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Not enough data for anomaly detection ({clean_df.shape[0]} rows).")
        return

    # Fit IsolationForest
    model = IsolationForest(contamination=contamination, random_state=42)
    preds = model.fit_predict(clean_df)

    # Map numeric predictions → human labels
    label_map = {1: "Normal", -1: "Anomaly"}
    clean_df["anomaly"] = [label_map[p] for p in preds]

    # Plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
    sns.scatterplot(
        data=clean_df,
        x=x,
        y=y,
        hue="anomaly",
        palette={"Normal": "blue", "Anomaly": "red"},
        alpha=0.6
    )

    plt.title(f"Anomaly Detection: {x} vs {y} — {clean_df.shape[0]} points")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 11. Lollipop Chart — Data-aware Top-N
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_lollipop(df, column, top_n=20):
    vc = df[column].value_counts()
    actual_n = min(top_n, len(vc))
    vc = vc.head(actual_n)

    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    plt.hlines(y=vc.index, xmin=0, xmax=vc.values, color='gray')
    plt.plot(vc.values, vc.index, "o", color="steelblue")

    plt.title(f"Lollipop Chart — Top {actual_n} {column}")
    plt.xlabel("Count")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 12. Treemap — Data-aware Top-N
# ------------------------------------------------------------
import squarify

def plot_treemap(df, column, top_n=20):
    vc = df[column].value_counts()
    actual_n = min(top_n, len(vc))
    vc = vc.head(actual_n)

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
    squarify.plot(
        sizes=vc.values,
        label=vc.index,
        alpha=0.8,
        color=sns.color_palette("viridis", actual_n)
    )
    plt.title(f"Treemap — Top {actual_n} {column}")
    plt.axis("off")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 13. Category Pareto — Data-aware Top-N
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_category_pareto(df, column, top_n=20):
    vc = df[column].value_counts()
    actual_n = min(top_n, len(vc))
    vc = vc.head(actual_n)
    cum_pct = vc.cumsum() / vc.sum()

    fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
    sns.barplot(x=vc.index, y=vc.values, ax=ax1, color="skyblue")
    ax1.set_ylabel("Count")
    ax1.set_xticklabels(ax1.get_xticklabels(), rotation=45, ha="right")

    ax2 = ax1.twinx()
    ax2.plot(vc.index, cum_pct, color="red", marker="o")
    ax2.set_ylabel("Cumulative %")

    plt.title(f"Pareto Chart — Top {actual_n} {column}")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 14. SAFE WRAPPER FOR CATEGORICAL PLOTS
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_categorical_safe(df, column, top_n=20):
    if column in df.columns:
        plot_categorical(df, column, top_n)
    else:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Column '{column}' not found in dataframe.")


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 15. Violin Plots (Comparative Distributions)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_violin(df, x, y, horizontal=True):
    """
    Violin plot for comparing the distribution of a numeric variable (y)
    across categories (x).

    Parameters
    ----------
    df : DataFrame
        Input dataframe.
    x : str
        Categorical column.
    y : str
        Numeric column.
    horizontal : bool, default True
        If True, flips the plot so categories are on the y-axis and
        numeric values on the x-axis. This improves readability for
        skewed numeric distributions and long category labels.
    """
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

    if horizontal:
        sns.violinplot(
            data=df,
            y=x,
            x=y,
            inner="quartile",
            cut=0
        )
        plt.xlabel(y)
        plt.ylabel(x)
        plt.title(f"Violin Plot — {y} by {x} (horizontal)")
    else:
        sns.violinplot(
            data=df,
            x=x,
            y=y,
            inner="quartile",
            cut=0
        )
        plt.xlabel(x)
        plt.ylabel(y)
        plt.title(f"Violin Plot — {y} by {x}")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 16. Pairplot (Multivariate Relationships)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_pairplot(df, cols, hue=None, title=None):
    """
    Pairplot for exploring multivariate relationships among selected columns.
    """
    g = sns.pairplot(df[cols].dropna(), hue=hue, corner=True, diag_kind="kde")
    if title:
        g.fig.suptitle(title, y=1.02)
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 17. Seasonal Decomposition (Hybrid, self-documented)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_seasonal_decomposition(
    df,
    date_col,
    value_col,
    dataset_type=None,
    freq=None,
    title=None
):
    """
    Seasonal decomposition for logistics and behavioural datasets.

    KEY DESIGN PRINCIPLES
    ----------------------
    1. **Seasonality requires one value per period**
       Seasonal decomposition cannot operate on raw transactional data
       (multiple rows per day). We therefore *aggregate first*, using
       resample().sum() to ensure a unique DatetimeIndex.

    2. **Frequency must match the seasonal cycle**
       - Logistics metrics (revenue, delays, volume) exhibit monthly or
         weekly seasonality → default to 'M' unless overridden.
       - Behavioural/session metrics often use daily or weekly cycles.

    3. **Model selection is domain‑aware**
       - Multiplicative → logistics metrics scale with volume
       - Additive → behavioural/session metrics with low variance
       If dataset_type is not provided, infer from column names.

    4. **Minimum data requirement**
       Seasonal decomposition requires at least *two full seasonal cycles*.
       For monthly data → 24+ months.

    5. **Self‑documenting behaviour**
       Every transformation is explicit and justified so the function is
       transparent, reproducible, and safe for production analytics.
    """

    # -----------------------------
    # 0. Validate inputs
    # -----------------------------
    if date_col not in df.columns or value_col not in df.columns:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] '{date_col}' or '{value_col}' not found.")
        return

    df_local = df.copy()
    df_local[date_col] = pd.to_datetime(df_local[date_col])
    df_local = df_local.sort_values(date_col)

    # Only numeric metrics can be decomposed
    if not pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df_local[value_col]):
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Column '{value_col}' is not numeric.")
        return

    # -----------------------------
    # 1. Frequency selection
    # -----------------------------
    # If user does not specify freq, choose domain‑appropriate default
    if freq is None:
        if dataset_type == "logistics":
            freq = "M"   # logistics seasonality is monthly
        elif dataset_type == "sessions":
            freq = "D"   # behavioural data often daily
        else:
            # fallback: infer from data, but override daily → monthly
            inferred = _infer_freq_from_dates(df_local, date_col)
            freq = "M" if inferred == "D" else inferred

    # -----------------------------
    # 2. Aggregate to one value per period
    # -----------------------------
    # CRITICAL FIX:
    #   seasonal_decompose CANNOT handle duplicate dates.
    #   We must aggregate using resample(), not asfreq().
    ts = df_local.set_index(date_col)[value_col].resample(freq).sum()

    # -----------------------------
    # 3. Determine seasonal period
    # -----------------------------
    # Map frequency → seasonal period length
    #   M → 12 (months in a year)
    #   W → 52 (weeks in a year)
    #   D → 7  (weekly seasonality)
    period_map = {"M": 12, "W": 52, "D": 7}
    period = period_map.get(freq, None)

    if period is None:
        print(f"[SKIPPED] Unsupported frequency '{freq}' for decomposition.")
        return

    # Need at least 2 full cycles
    if ts.dropna().shape[0] < period * 2:
        print(
            f"[SKIPPED] Not enough data for seasonal decomposition "
            f"(need ≥ {2*period} aggregated periods, have {ts.dropna().shape[0]})."
        )
        return

    # -----------------------------
    # 4. Model selection
    # -----------------------------
    if dataset_type is None:
        dataset_type = _infer_dataset_type(df_local)

    if dataset_type == "logistics":
        model = "multiplicative"
    elif dataset_type == "sessions":
        model = "additive"
    else:
        model = "additive"

    # -----------------------------
    # 5. Perform decomposition
    # -----------------------------
    result = seasonal_decompose(
        ts,
        model=model,
        period=period,
        extrapolate_trend="freq"
    )

    # -----------------------------
    # 6. Plot results
    # -----------------------------
    fig = result.plot()
    fig.set_size_inches(12, 8)

    auto_title = (
        f"Seasonal Decomposition of {value_col} "
        f"(model='{model}', freq='{freq}', period={period})"
    )
    fig.suptitle(title or auto_title, y=1.02)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()



# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 18. Category Alignment (View share vs Revenue share)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_category_alignment(df_views, df_revenue, category_col, views_col, revenue_col, top_n=20):
    """
    Compares category view share vs revenue share.

    - df_views:    dataset with view counts per category
    - df_revenue:  dataset with revenue per category
    - category_col: common category column
    - views_col:    column with view counts
    - revenue_col:  column with revenue

    Produces a side-by-side bar chart of shares.
    """
    views = (
        df_views.groupby(category_col)[views_col]
        .sum()
        .sort_values(ascending=False)
    )
    revenue = (
        df_revenue.groupby(category_col)[revenue_col]
        .sum()
        .sort_values(ascending=False)
    )

    common = views.index.intersection(revenue.index)
    views = views.loc[common]
    revenue = revenue.loc[common]

    views = views.head(top_n)
    revenue = revenue.loc[views.index]

    views_share = views / views.sum()
    revenue_share = revenue / revenue.sum()

    plot_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "views_share": views_share,
        "revenue_share": revenue_share
    })

    plot_df = plot_df.sort_values("revenue_share", ascending=False)

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    plot_df[["views_share", "revenue_share"]].plot(kind="bar", ax=plt.gca())
    plt.title(f"Category Alignment — View Share vs Revenue Share (Top {len(plot_df)})")
    plt.ylabel("Share")
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 19. Top Viewed vs Top Purchased Products
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_top_viewed_vs_purchased(df_views, df_purchases, product_col, views_col, qty_col, top_n=20):
    """
    Compares top viewed vs top purchased products.

    - df_views:    dataset with product view counts
    - df_purchases: dataset with product purchase quantities
    - product_col: common product identifier
    - views_col:   column with view counts
    - qty_col:     column with purchase quantities
    """
    views = (
        df_views.groupby(product_col)[views_col]
        .sum()
        .sort_values(ascending=False)
        .head(top_n)
    )

    purchases = (
        df_purchases.groupby(product_col)[qty_col]
        .sum()
        .sort_values(ascending=False)
    )

    common = views.index.intersection(purchases.index)
    views = views.loc[common]
    purchases = purchases.loc[common]

    plot_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "views": views,
        "purchases": purchases.loc[views.index]
    })

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    plot_df.plot(kind="bar", ax=plt.gca())
    plt.title(f"Top Viewed vs Top Purchased Products (Top {len(plot_df)})")
    plt.ylabel("Count")
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 20. Scatter Map (Dynamic lat/lon or country fallback)
# ------------------------------------------------------------
def plot_scatter_map(df, size_col=None, color_col=None):
    """
    Geographic scatter visualisation with normalized coordinate scaling.

    WHY NORMALIZATION IS NECESSARY:
    -------------------------------
    Real-world latitude and longitude ranges can be extremely unbalanced.
    In this dataset, longitude spans ~273 degrees while latitude spans ~82 degrees.
    If plotted directly, the map becomes a long horizontal strip and appears compressed.

    To fix this, we normalize both axes to a 0–1 range:
    - This produces a visually balanced scatter plot.
    - It removes extreme stretching caused by real-world coordinate ratios.
    - It makes clusters, patterns, and delivery-status differences readable.

    This approach is standard when the goal is *pattern visualization* rather than
    geographic accuracy.
    """

    lat_col, lon_col = _detect_lat_lon_columns(df)
    country_col = _detect_country_column(df)

    # ---------------------------------------------------------
    # CASE 1 — LAT/LON SCATTER MAP WITH NORMALIZED COORDINATES
    # ---------------------------------------------------------
    if lat_col and lon_col:

        df = df.copy()

        # Compute min/max
        lat_min, lat_max = df[lat_col].min(), df[lat_col].max()
        lon_min, lon_max = df[lon_col].min(), df[lon_col].max()

        # Normalize to 0–1 range
        df["lat_norm"] = (df[lat_col] - lat_min) / (lat_max - lat_min)
        df["lon_norm"] = (df[lon_col] - lon_min) / (lon_max - lon_min)

        # Optional jitter for repeated coordinates
        if df[lat_col].nunique() < 50 or df[lon_col].nunique() < 50:
            df["lat_norm"] += np.random.uniform(-0.01, 0.01, size=len(df))
            df["lon_norm"] += np.random.uniform(-0.01, 0.01, size=len(df))

        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

        sns.scatterplot(
            data=df,
            x="lon_norm",
            y="lat_norm",
            size=size_col,
            hue=color_col,
            alpha=0.5,
            palette="viridis",
            edgecolor="none"
        )

        plt.title("Geographic Scatter Map (Normalized Scaling)")
        plt.xlabel("Longitude (normalized)")
        plt.ylabel("Latitude (normalized)")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()
        return

    # ---------------------------------------------------------
    # CASE 2 — FALLBACK: COUNTRY BAR CHART
    # ---------------------------------------------------------
    if country_col:
        vc = df[country_col].value_counts().head(20)
        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
        sns.barplot(x=vc.values, y=vc.index, palette="viridis")
        plt.title(f"Top {len(vc)} Countries (Fallback — No Coordinates)")
        plt.xlabel("Count")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()
        return

    # ---------------------------------------------------------
    # CASE 3 — NOTHING FOUND
    # ---------------------------------------------------------
    print("[SKIPPED] No geographic columns detected (lat/lon or country).")


def plot_geo_hexbin(df, lat_col="store_latitude", lon_col="store_longitude",
                    gridsize=40, cmap="viridis"):
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))

    plt.hexbin(
        df[lon_col],
        df[lat_col],
        gridsize=gridsize,
        cmap=cmap,
        mincnt=1,
        bins="log"
    )

    plt.colorbar(label="Log Density")
    plt.xlabel("Longitude")
    plt.ylabel("Latitude")
    plt.title("Geospatial Hexbin Density Map")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


def plot_geo_kde(df, lat_col="store_latitude", lon_col="store_longitude"):
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))

    sns.kdeplot(
        x=df[lon_col],
        y=df[lat_col],
        fill=True,
        cmap="mako",
        thresh=0.05,
        levels=50
    )

    plt.xlabel("Longitude")
    plt.ylabel("Latitude")
    plt.title("Geospatial KDE Density Map")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


def plot_geo_hist2d(df, lat_col="store_latitude", lon_col="store_longitude",
                    bins=50, cmap="viridis"):
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))

    plt.hist2d(
        df[lon_col],
        df[lat_col],
        bins=bins,
        cmap=cmap
    )

    plt.colorbar(label="Count")
    plt.xlabel("Longitude")
    plt.ylabel("Latitude")
    plt.title("Geospatial 2D Histogram Map")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

def plot_geo_hexbin_status(df,
                           lat_col="store_latitude",
                           lon_col="store_longitude",
                           status_col="delivery_status",
                           gridsize=40):
    """
    Hexbin map with delivery-status overlay.

    PURPOSE
    -------
    This map shows:
    - True geographic density (hexbin)
    - Operational performance overlay (delivery status)
    - Real lat/lon preserved (no normalization)
    - No external APIs required

    WHY THIS WORKS
    --------------
    - Hexbin aggregates dense regions → reveals hotspots clearly.
    - Overlaying delivery status shows where late deliveries cluster.
    - Using separate layers avoids scatterplot overplotting.
    - Works even with skewed lat/lon ranges.
    """

    # Define colors for delivery status
    status_palette = {
        "Advance shipping": "navy",
        "Shipping on time": "cyan",
        "Late delivery": "red",
        "Shipping canceled": "orange"
    }

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))

    # Base hexbin layer (density)
    hb = plt.hexbin(
        df[lon_col],
        df[lat_col],
        gridsize=gridsize,
        cmap="Greys",
        mincnt=1,
        bins="log",
        alpha=0.5
    )
    plt.colorbar(hb, label="Log Density")

    # Overlay delivery status as scatter
    for status, color in status_palette.items():
        subset = df[df[status_col] == status]
        plt.scatter(
            subset[lon_col],
            subset[lat_col],
            s=10,
            color=color,
            alpha=0.6,
            label=status
        )

    plt.xlabel("Longitude")
    plt.ylabel("Latitude")
    plt.title("Geospatial Hexbin Map with Delivery-Status Overlay")
    plt.legend(title="Delivery Status", loc="upper right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


def plot_conversion_scatter(df_views, df_purchases, product_col, views_col, qty_col):
    # Aggregate
    views = df_views.groupby(product_col)[views_col].sum()
    purchases = df_purchases.groupby(product_col)[qty_col].sum()

    # Align products
    common = views.index.intersection(purchases.index)
    df = pd.DataFrame({
        "views": views.loc[common],
        "purchases": purchases.loc[common]
    })

    # Plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
    sns.scatterplot(data=df, x="views", y="purchases", alpha=0.7)

    # Diagonal reference line
    max_val = max(df["views"].max(), df["purchases"].max())
    plt.plot([0, max_val], [0, max_val], linestyle="--", color="gray")

    plt.title("Conversion Scatter: Views vs Purchases")
    plt.xlabel("Product Views")
    plt.ylabel("Product Purchases")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

def plot_rank_rank(df_views, df_purchases, product_col, views_col, qty_col):
    # Aggregate
    views = df_views.groupby(product_col)[views_col].sum().rank(ascending=False)
    purchases = df_purchases.groupby(product_col)[qty_col].sum().rank(ascending=False)

    # Align
    common = views.index.intersection(purchases.index)
    df = pd.DataFrame({
        "view_rank": views.loc[common],
        "purchase_rank": purchases.loc[common]
    })

    # Plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
    sns.scatterplot(data=df, x="view_rank", y="purchase_rank", alpha=0.7)

    # Diagonal reference line
    max_rank = max(df["view_rank"].max(), df["purchase_rank"].max())
    plt.plot([1, max_rank], [1, max_rank], linestyle="--", color="gray")

    plt.title("Rank–Rank Comparison: Viewed vs Purchased")
    plt.xlabel("Rank by Views (1 = most viewed)")
    plt.ylabel("Rank by Purchases (1 = most purchased)")
    plt.gca().invert_xaxis()
    plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

### 1. DISTRIBUTION & SHAPE OF KEY NUMERICAL VARIABLES

#### Revenue-related distributions

##### Revenue Concentration and the Case for Log Transformation

```{python}
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "net_sales_amount")
```

The distribution reflects a classic heavy‑tailed revenue pattern where most orders fall within a narrow low‑value range, while a long, slowly declining tail extends toward higher transaction amounts. This indicates that the majority of sales are small, but a minority of large orders meaningfully stretch the upper range and shape the overall distribution. The steep rise near zero and gradual decay across the x‑axis confirm strong right‑skewness, making the distribution incompatible with symmetric assumptions such as normality.

The presence of extreme values means that averages, variance, and scale‑sensitive models can be disproportionately influenced by a small number of high‑value transactions. This behaviour is typical in retail and e‑commerce settings, where high‑value orders are rare but impactful.

A log transformation is well‑justified in this context. Applying `log(net_sales_amount + 1)` compresses extreme values, reduces skewness, and produces a more stable, interpretable distribution. This transformation improves the performance of linear models, stabilises variance, and reduces the dominance of outliers, allowing downstream modelling to focus on meaningful structural relationships rather than being distorted by a handful of large orders.

##### Line‑Level Value Concentration and the Structural Case for Log Transformation

```{python}
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "line_total_amount")
```

The distribution shows the same structural signature observed in `net_sales_amount`: a dense concentration of small values and a long, gradually thinning right tail. Most line totals fall between 0 and roughly 500, with a clear peak in the low‑hundreds. As values increase, frequencies decline sharply, and only a small minority of transactions extend toward the upper end of the range. This produces a strongly right‑skewed, heavy‑tailed pattern.

Because line totals represent individual items rather than full orders, the concentration near zero is even more pronounced than in net sales. Net sales aggregate multiple line totals, so its tail extends further, but both variables reflect the same underlying commercial behaviour: many low‑value lines and a small number of high‑value ones. The shared skewness confirms that the distributional shape is intrinsic to the business process rather than an artefact of data quality.

This shape has practical implications. A small set of high‑value lines disproportionately influences averages, variance, and any model sensitive to scale or outliers. The asymmetry also violates assumptions of normality and homoscedasticity that many statistical and machine‑learning models rely on.

A log transformation is well‑suited here. Applying `log(line_total_amount + 1)` compresses extreme values, reduces skewness, and produces a more stable, interpretable distribution. It also aligns the scale of line totals more closely with net sales, improving coherence across features and supporting more robust downstream modelling.

##### Revenue Concentration, Heavy‑Tail Dynamics, and the Case for Log Transformation

```{python}
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "revenue" )
```



The distribution shows a dense cluster of low‑revenue orders followed by a long, gradually tapering right tail. This pattern indicates that most transactions generate relatively small revenue, while a much smaller subset contributes disproportionately large amounts. The heavy tail is economically meaningful: high‑value orders are rare but exert strong influence on the overall distribution, stretching the upper range and pulling the mean upward. As a result, the “typical” order is far smaller than what the average suggests.

This asymmetry has direct analytical implications. The dominance of low‑value orders combined with a minority of extreme values creates a distribution that violates assumptions of symmetry and constant variance. Models that rely on normality or homoscedasticity will struggle, and summary statistics such as the mean or standard deviation will be heavily influenced by outliers rather than representative behaviour.

A log transformation is well suited for this variable. Applying `log(revenue + 1)` compresses extreme values, reduces skewness, and produces a more balanced, stable distribution. This transformation improves interpretability, stabilises variance, and makes relationships with other features more linear and model‑friendly. It also ensures that downstream modelling focuses on structural patterns rather than being dominated by a small number of high‑value transactions.

#### Discounts & profit ratios

##### Extreme Discount Outliers and Their Operational and Modelling Implications

```{python}
# Discounts & profit ratios
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "order_item_discount")
plot_box(fact_logistics_gold, "order_item_discount")
```

The distribution shows a dense concentration of very small discounts, followed by a long stretch of unusually large values. The boxplot reinforces this pattern: the interquartile range is tightly compressed near zero, the upper whisker rises only modestly, and then a large number of individual points extend far beyond it. This structure signals the presence of extreme outliers—discounts that are far larger than what the underlying distribution would naturally produce.

Several explanations can account for these extremes. Some may stem from operational or data‑entry anomalies, such as manual overrides, bulk adjustments, or inconsistent application of discount rules. Others may reflect legitimate policy exceptions, including clearance events, special customer tiers, or product‑specific promotions. Mixed discount mechanisms can also create artificial outliers if percentage‑based and absolute‑value discounts are stored in the same field. In some cases, the highest values may indicate data quality issues such as mis‑scaled entries, duplicated discounts, or incorrect currency conversions.

From a modelling perspective, these outliers can distort averages, inflate variance, and weaken relationships with revenue, margin, or product attributes. A log transformation helps stabilise the distribution by compressing extreme values and reducing skewness, but direct inspection of the largest discounts is still advisable. Understanding whether they represent genuine business behaviour or require cleaning or capping will improve both interpretability and downstream model performance.

```{python}
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "order_item_discount_rate")
plot_box(fact_logistics_gold, "order_item_discount_rate")
```

The discount rate distribution is tightly concentrated in a narrow band, with most values clustered between roughly 0.05 and 0.15 and a clear peak around the midpoint of that range. The histogram shows a steep rise near the lower end, followed by a smooth decline, which signals a right‑skewed distribution but with far less extreme tail behaviour than the absolute discount amounts. The boxplot reinforces this: the interquartile range is compact, the median sits cleanly inside it, and the whiskers extend only modestly before reaching the maximum. There are no extreme spikes or long chains of outliers, which means discount rates are applied in a more controlled and consistent way.

This pattern suggests that a relatively small subset of transactions does not dominate the discount rate landscape. Instead, the business appears to operate with a fairly standardised discounting policy, where most items receive similar percentage reductions. The absence of extreme outliers implies that discount rates are likely governed by rules or automated logic rather than manual overrides. This contrasts with absolute discount amounts, where large outliers can arise from high‑priced items or operational inconsistencies.

From an analytical perspective, the stability of the discount rate distribution makes it a more reliable feature for modelling than raw discount amounts. It is less sensitive to product price variation and less prone to distortion from rare, high‑value transactions. The narrow range also means transformations are generally unnecessary, and the variable can be used directly in models without risking undue influence from a small number of extreme cases.

```{python}
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "order_profit_per_order")
plot_box(fact_logistics_gold, "order_profit_per_order")
```

Profitability patterns and risk signals
The distribution shows that most orders cluster very close to zero profit, with a substantial portion dipping into negative territory. This creates a left‑skewed shape where losses extend into a long, heavy tail. That tail indicates that while small losses are common, a meaningful number of orders incur large negative margins, and these extreme cases stretch far beyond the typical range. The boxplot reinforces this pattern: the central box is narrow and positioned near zero, while a dense chain of outliers extends deep into the negative side, signalling frequent and sometimes severe unprofitable transactions.

Several risks emerge from this structure. A concentration of orders near break‑even suggests thin margins overall, leaving little buffer for operational variability. The heavy negative tail implies that certain products, customer segments, or fulfilment scenarios systematically generate losses, which can distort financial performance and mask underlying inefficiencies. These extreme negative values also inflate variance, making profitability harder to model and predict. They may reflect legitimate business strategy—such as loss‑leader items or aggressive discounting—but they can also point to data quality issues, pricing misalignment, or fulfilment cost overruns.

From an analytical standpoint, the combination of skewness and heavy tails makes raw profit values unstable for modelling. A transformation such as log‑scaling (applied to shifted values) or winsorisation can help stabilise variance and reduce the influence of extreme losses. More importantly, the tail warrants targeted investigation to determine whether the negative outliers represent intentional strategy, operational leakage, or data anomalies.

#### Shipping delays

```{python}
# Shipping delays
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "shipping_days_actual")
plot_numeric_dist(fact_logistics_gold, "shipping_delay")
```

Interpretation:  
Shipping duration patterns and operational implications
The distribution of actual shipping days shows a clear centre of gravity around two days, with smaller but consistent peaks at three, four, five, and six days. This pattern indicates a typical delivery window of 2–4 days, with most orders falling into a predictable operational rhythm. The small peak at zero days likely reflects same‑day fulfilment or local pickup scenarios rather than true zero‑day shipping.

The delay distribution reinforces this structure. Distinct peaks at integer values—especially at 1, 0, and −2—suggest that delays tend to occur in discrete steps rather than as a continuous spread. A delay of one day appears to be the most common deviation, while negative delays (early deliveries) also occur frequently enough to form their own mode. The presence of both early and late deliveries indicates variability in fulfilment efficiency, carrier performance, or customer proximity.

Operationally, the combination of tight clustering and stepped delays points to a system that is generally consistent but subject to systematic rather than random deviations. Regular one‑day delays may reflect bottlenecks in packing, handoff to carriers, or regional processing capacity. Early deliveries may indicate overestimation in scheduled times or faster‑than‑expected carrier performance. The absence of a long right tail is encouraging: extreme delays are rare, which reduces customer dissatisfaction risk and stabilises service‑level metrics.

From an analytics perspective, the stepped nature of delays suggests that modelling should treat delay as an ordinal or discrete variable rather than assuming continuous behaviour. It also highlights the value of segmenting by region, carrier, and product type to identify where the one‑day delay mode is most pronounced. Understanding these patterns can help target operational improvements and refine delivery‑time predictions.

#### Session-level behaviour

```{python}
# Session-level behaviour
plot_numeric_dist(sessions_features_gold, "page_views")
plot_numeric_dist(sessions_features_gold, "unique_url_actions")
plot_numeric_dist(sessions_features_gold, "key_event_count")
plot_numeric_dist(sessions_features_gold, "duration_seconds", log_scale=True)
```

Interpretation:  
Engagement patterns and session duration
The distribution shows that most sessions are short, with page views, unique URL actions, and key events all clustering near the lower end of their respective scales. Each metric exhibits a steep drop‑off as counts increase, which signals a strong engagement skew: a large volume of low‑interaction sessions and a progressively smaller number of highly engaged ones. This is typical of web behaviour, where casual browsing dominates and only a minority of users explore deeply or trigger meaningful events.

The duration distribution reinforces this pattern. Even on a log scale, most sessions sit at the lower end, while a long right tail stretches toward much longer durations. These long‑lived sessions represent a small but important subset of users who spend significantly more time on the site. They often correspond to high‑intent behaviour—product comparison, research, or multi‑step journeys—and can be disproportionately valuable for conversion modelling.

Implications for modelling
Heavy skew requires transformation. Raw counts and durations will produce unstable variance and can dominate model behaviour. Log or square‑root transformations help stabilise these metrics and make relationships more linear.

Long‑tail users should be treated as a distinct behavioural segment. Their patterns differ meaningfully from the majority, and models that assume a single homogeneous distribution may underperform.

Engagement metrics are not interchangeable. Page views, unique actions, and key events each decay at different rates, suggesting they capture different layers of user intent. Feature engineering should preserve these distinctions rather than collapsing them into a single score.

Duration is especially sensitive to outliers. Even after log transformation, the longest sessions may require capping or winsorisation to prevent undue influence on predictive models.

#### Product Prices

```{python}
# Product prices (assuming avg_price or similar exists)
plot_box(product_features_gold, "avg_price")
```

#### Interpretation of Price Range and Product Positioning

The distribution of product prices reveals a strategy heavily skewed toward the "entry-to-mid-level" market, with a long tail of premium offerings that define the catalog's aspirational limit:

- **The Volume Core ($0–$250):** The vast majority of the product catalog is concentrated under the **$250 mark**. This dense cluster represents high-velocity, "commodity" items where competition is likely based on volume and fulfillment speed. Within this range, there is a sub-peak around **$50–$100**, suggesting a "sweet spot" for impulsive or routine consumer purchases.

- **The Mid-Tier Gap ($250–$600):** There is a noticeable thinning of product density as prices move toward the $500 mark. This suggests the business may have a "missing middle"—a lack of "prosumer" or mid-tier lifestyle products that bridge the gap between basic sports gear and professional-grade equipment.

- **The Premium Tier ($600–$2,000):** The catalog features a sparse but consistent "long tail" extending up to nearly **$2,000**. These items are likely technical equipment (e.g., high-end bicycles, professional treadmills, or premium electronics). While they represent a small fraction of total unit volume, they are critical for maintaining the brand's positioning as a serious sporting goods provider.

- **Strategic Positioning Assessment:** - **Market Role:** The business is positioned as an **accessible mass-market retailer**. 
    - **Risk:** With such a high concentration in the low-price bracket, the business is extremely sensitive to the **margin erosion** and **shipping delays** identified in previous analyses. A $10 margin hit on a $50 item is catastrophic (20% loss), whereas the same hit on a $1,000 item is negligible.
    - **Opportunity:** Diversifying the **$300–$700 range** could improve overall Average Order Value (AOV) without requiring the high-touch sales effort associated with the $1,500+ premium tier.

- **Pricing Granularity:** The price points appear highly standardized (noted by the "spikes" in the distribution), suggesting a rigid pricing architecture rather than dynamic, market-responsive pricing. This rigidity might explain why aggressive discounting is often needed to move inventory that doesn't hit these pre-set psychological price points.

---

**Summary:** The catalog is optimized for **accessibility and high turnover**. However, the extreme density at the low end of the price spectrum makes the business model vulnerable to operational inefficiencies, as there is very little "price buffer" to absorb the high rates of late delivery and heavy discounting observed elsewhere in the data.

#### Violin plot: revenue by department

```{python}
# Violin plots: revenue by department, shipping delay by region, engagement by day of week
plot_violin(fact_logistics_gold, x="department_name", y="revenue", horizontal=True)
plot_box_categorical(fact_logistics_gold, category="department_name", value="revenue", horizontal=True)
```

##### Revenue patterns across departments

The boxplot and violin plot together show that revenue is **highly unevenly distributed** across departments. Several departments (Book Shop, Pet Shop, Discs Shop) have tightly clustered revenue with low medians and narrow spreads, indicating **low variance and predictable performance**. Others—especially Technology, Footwear, Outdoors, and Golf—show **wide revenue ranges**, many outliers, and long right‑tails, signalling **high‑variance revenue behaviour** driven by a mix of low‑value and occasional high‑value transactions.

Key high‑variance areas include:
- **Technology** — the widest spread, many extreme outliers, and a long tail of high‑value orders.
- **Footwear** — broad distribution with multiple high‑revenue points.
- **Outdoors & Golf** — moderate medians but large spreads, suggesting occasional high-ticket items.
- **Health & Beauty** — mid‑range median but noticeable variability.

Low‑variance departments include:
- **Book Shop**
- **Pet Shop**
- **Discs Shop**
These show compact distributions with few outliers, indicating stable, low‑value revenue patterns.

---

##### Why the violin plot struggled to scale

The violin plot uses **kernel density estimation (KDE)** to show the full shape of the distribution. KDE assumes a smooth, continuous distribution and tries to stretch across the entire numeric range. Revenue, however, is:

- **Extremely right‑skewed**
- **Heavy‑tailed**
- **Full of extreme outliers**
- **Highly uneven across categories**

This causes the KDE to stretch dramatically to accommodate the largest values (e.g., Technology’s high‑revenue outliers). As a result:

- The violins become **thin and compressed** for low‑variance departments.
- The density collapses near zero for most categories.
- The plot loses meaningful shape because the KDE is dominated by a few extreme values.
- The visual becomes **unbalanced**, with one or two departments dictating the entire x‑axis scale.

In short, the violin plot cannot display the true distribution shape when the data is **too skewed and too sparse at the high end**.

---

##### Why the boxplot handles this variable better

The boxplot is **robust to skewness and outliers** because it summarizes the distribution using quartiles and whiskers rather than density estimation. This makes it ideal for revenue:

- It clearly shows **median differences** across departments.
- It highlights **IQR width**, making variance comparisons straightforward.
- Outliers are shown as points without distorting the entire scale.
- The shape is not dependent on KDE smoothing, so extreme values don’t collapse the plot.

For heavy‑tailed variables like revenue, the boxplot provides a **clean, interpretable, and stable** view of cross‑department differences.

#### Violin plot: shipping delay by region

```{python}
plot_violin(fact_logistics_gold, x="destination_region", y="shipping_delay")
plot_box_categorical(fact_logistics_gold, category="destination_region", value="shipping_delay", horizontal=True)
```

##### Regions with consistently higher delays

Several regions show delay distributions shifted toward **positive values**, meaning late deliveries occur more frequently and with greater spread. These regions combine both higher average delays and wider variability:

- **Central Africa, West Africa, East Africa** — the widest violins, with density concentrated around +1 to +3 days and long spreads. This pattern reflects structural constraints such as customs processing, long-haul routing, and inconsistent carrier performance.
- **South America** — a broad distribution with a noticeable right shift, indicating recurring late deliveries and operational unpredictability.
- **Central Asia and South Asia** — moderate but consistent positive skew, suggesting systemic bottlenecks in cross-border logistics or long transit chains.

These regions represent the **highest operational risk**, where delays are both common and volatile.

---

##### Regions with stable or early deliveries

Some regions cluster tightly around zero or even show density in negative values (early deliveries):

- **Western Europe, Northern Europe, Eastern Europe** — narrow, centered violins with minimal spread, indicating highly predictable delivery performance.
- **US Center, East of USA, West of USA** — compact distributions with slight negative skew, reflecting efficient domestic logistics and reliable carrier networks.
- **Canada** — moderate spread but still centered near zero, showing stable cross-border operations.

These regions exhibit **low variance and high reliability**, making them operationally low-risk.

---

##### Regions with the highest variability

Variability is visible where the violin widens across multiple delay values:

- **Central Africa, West Africa, East Africa** — the broadest shapes, spanning early to multi-day delays.
- **South America and Central Asia** — wide distributions with no dominant mode, indicating inconsistent transit performance.
- **Oceania** — moderate delays but a long spread, reflecting long-distance shipping and carrier variability.

High variance complicates forecasting, SLA commitments, and customer communication.

---

##### Operational implications

- **SLA exposure** is highest in regions with wide, right-skewed violins; these areas are more likely to breach delivery promises.
- **Carrier and route optimization** is most impactful in Africa, South America, and Central Asia, where delays appear systemic rather than random.
- **Inventory positioning** may need to shift toward regional hubs or forward-stocking to stabilize delivery times.
- **Customer ETA messaging** should be dynamic in high-variance regions, while stable regions can support tighter delivery windows.

Understanding these patterns helps prioritize where operational improvements will yield the greatest reliability gains.

#### Violin plot: engagement by day of week

```{python}
plot_violin(sessions_features_gold, x="session_day_of_week", y="engagement_score")
```

##### Engagement patterns across the week

The distribution of engagement scores shows that most days share a broadly similar pattern, with a dense concentration of low‑to‑moderate engagement and a long right tail representing a smaller group of highly engaged sessions. Even with this shared structure, the violins reveal meaningful differences in both central tendency and variability across days.

- **Tuesday and Wednesday** show the widest violins, indicating a larger spread of engagement scores and a higher density of mid‑to‑high engagement sessions. These days appear to support deeper browsing or more intentional user activity.
- **Friday and Monday** display moderate variability, with a noticeable presence of higher engagement values but a more compact core distribution. These days likely reflect mixed behaviour—routine weekday usage with occasional spikes.
- **Thursday and Saturday** show narrower distributions, suggesting more consistent but generally lower engagement. These days may reflect lighter browsing patterns or shorter sessions.
- **Sunday** has one of the tightest distributions, with fewer high‑engagement outliers, indicating more uniform and typically lower engagement behaviour.

---

##### Peak engagement days

The strongest engagement signals appear on:

- **Tuesday**
- **Wednesday**

Both days show:
- Wider distributions
- Higher density in the upper engagement range
- More pronounced tails of high‑engagement sessions

This suggests that mid‑week activity is not only more frequent but also more intensive, with users more likely to explore, interact, or complete deeper journeys.

---

##### Interpretation and operational implications

- **Mid‑week optimization**: Tuesday and Wednesday are prime candidates for launching content, campaigns, or feature rollouts that benefit from higher engagement.
- **Weekend behaviour**: Saturday and Sunday show more uniform, lower engagement, which may reflect casual browsing rather than high‑intent activity.
- **User segmentation**: The long right tails on high‑engagement days indicate a subset of power users whose behaviour may be worth modelling separately.
- **Predictive modelling**: Day‑of‑week should be included as a categorical feature, as it captures meaningful behavioural variance and improves session‑level predictions.

Understanding these patterns helps align engagement strategies with natural user rhythms and supports more accurate behavioural modelling.

### 2. CATEGORICAL DISTRIBUTIONS & CONCENTRATION PATTERNS

#### Scale of late vs on‑time deliveries

```{python}
plot_categorical(fact_logistics_gold, "delivery_status")
```

The distribution shows a clear dominance of late deliveries relative to on‑time performance. Using the approximate counts:

- **Late delivery ≈ 95,000**
- **Shipping on time ≈ 40,000**
- **Advance shipping ≈ 45,000**
- **Shipping canceled ≈ 10,000**

Late deliveries alone exceed the on‑time category by a large margin. The ratio of late to on‑time deliveries is roughly:

- **Late vs On‑time ≈ 95,000 / 40,000 ≈ 2.4×**

This means late deliveries occur **more than twice as often** as on‑time deliveries.

When comparing late deliveries to the combined volume of all “successful” outcomes (advance + on‑time):

- **Advance + On‑time ≈ 85,000**
- **Late ≈ 95,000**

Even together, early and on‑time shipments **still fall short** of the late‑delivery count.

---

##### Interpretation and operational implications

- Late deliveries represent the **single largest delivery outcome**, indicating a systemic reliability issue rather than isolated exceptions.
- The gap between late and on‑time deliveries suggests **persistent bottlenecks** in fulfillment, carrier performance, or regional logistics.
- The high volume of advance shipments highlights **variability** rather than consistent performance, reinforcing the need for tighter process control.
- SLA compliance, customer satisfaction, and forecasting accuracy are all likely impacted by this imbalance.

Understanding which regions, carriers, or product types contribute most to late deliveries would help target the most impactful operational improvements.

#### Transaction Types

```{python}
plot_categorical(fact_logistics_gold, "transaction_type")
```

##### Distribution of transaction types

The mix of transaction types shows a clear hierarchy in operational volume, with one category dominating and the others forming a secondary tier. The approximate counts are:

- **DEBIT ≈ 70,000** — the largest category by a wide margin, representing the core transactional load.
- **TRANSFER ≈ 50,000** — substantial volume, forming the second‑largest operational stream.
- **PAYMENT ≈ 42,000** — close to transfers, indicating steady and frequent usage.
- **CASH ≈ 20,000** — the smallest category, less than one‑third of debit volume.

This distribution indicates that the system is primarily driven by **electronic, account‑based activity**, with cash transactions forming a much smaller share.

---

##### Operational focus implied by the mix

- **Debit transactions dominate workload**. With nearly 70k occurrences, debit operations likely drive the majority of system throughput, error handling, and performance considerations. Any optimization in this category yields the highest operational impact.
- **Transfers and payments form a strong mid‑tier**. Their combined volume (~92k) rivals debit activity, suggesting that inter‑account movement and bill‑payment workflows are also central to user behaviour.
- **Cash transactions are niche**. Their lower volume implies limited operational strain but may require specialized handling due to physical or compliance‑related processes.
- **Electronic-first behaviour**. The dominance of debit, transfer, and payment categories indicates a user base that relies heavily on digital financial interactions rather than cash‑based workflows.

---

##### Strategic implications

- **System reliability and scaling** should prioritize debit and transfer pipelines, as disruptions here would affect the majority of users.
- **Fraud monitoring** may need differentiated rules: debit and transfer categories carry higher exposure due to volume and transaction value patterns.
- **User experience improvements** should focus on debit and payment flows, where even small friction reductions can meaningfully improve overall satisfaction.
- **Cash workflows** may be candidates for automation or simplification, given their lower frequency but potentially higher handling cost.

Understanding how these transaction types evolve over time—especially shifts between debit, transfer, and payment—can help anticipate operational load and guide resource allocation.

#### Distribution of Customer Segments

```{python}
plot_categorical(fact_logistics_gold, "customer_segment")
plot_categorical(fact_logistics_gold, "department_name")
plot_categorical(fact_logistics_gold, "category_name")
```

##### Dominant customer segments

The customer mix is heavily concentrated in one segment:

- **Consumer** — the largest segment by a wide margin, forming the core of the customer base.
- **Corporate** — a meaningful secondary segment, but far smaller than Consumer.
- **Home Office** — the smallest segment, representing a niche portion of demand.

This distribution indicates that the business is primarily **B2C‑driven**, with B2B activity present but not dominant.

---

##### Dominant departments

Across departments, a few categories clearly lead in volume:

- **Fan Shop** — the highest‑volume department, suggesting strong demand for fan‑related merchandise.
- **Apparel** — a major contributor, reflecting broad and consistent consumer interest.
- **Golf** — another high‑volume department, indicating a strong sports‑oriented customer base.

Lower‑volume departments such as **Book Shop**, **Health and Beauty**, and **Pet Shop** play smaller, more specialized roles.

This mix shows a product ecosystem centered around **sports, apparel, and fan merchandise**, with niche categories supplementing the assortment.

---

##### Dominant product categories

Within the top 20 product categories, several stand out as clear leaders:

- **Consumer Electronics** — the single largest category, significantly ahead of all others.
- **Men’s Clothing**, **Women’s Clothing**, **Women’s Footwear**, and **Indoor Games** — strong mid‑tier categories with high counts.
- Additional notable categories include **Men’s Footwear**, **Golf Clubs**, **Sporting Goods**, and **Kids’ Clothing**.

The category distribution highlights a broad but uneven product landscape, with **electronics and apparel** forming the backbone of demand, supported by **sports and recreation** categories.

---

##### Combined interpretation

Across segments, departments, and categories, a consistent pattern emerges:

- The customer base is **consumer‑heavy**.
- The strongest departments revolve around **sports, apparel, and fan merchandise**.
- The most dominant product category is **Consumer Electronics**, indicating high‑value, high‑volume demand.
- Apparel and sports categories reinforce the same theme: a customer base oriented toward **lifestyle, fashion, and recreational activities**.

This alignment suggests that operational focus, inventory planning, and marketing strategy should prioritize **consumer electronics, apparel, and sports‑related products**, as these areas drive the majority of volume and engagement.

### Pareto Concentration

```{python}
# Pareto concentration
plot_pareto(fact_logistics_gold, "category_name", horizontal=False)
plot_pareto(fact_logistics_gold, "destination_country", horizontal=False)
plot_pareto(fact_logistics_gold, "store_city", horizontal=False)
```

##### 80/20 concentration across categories, countries, and cities

The Pareto charts show a classic **80/20 distribution**, where a small number of categories or locations account for the majority of total volume. The cumulative curves rise steeply in the first few bars and then flatten, indicating that most activity is concentrated in a narrow set of high‑frequency items.

---

##### Where most **volume** is concentrated (category_name)

A handful of product categories dominate total volume:

- **Cleats, Men’s Footwear, Women’s Apparel, Indoor/Outdoor Games** form the steep initial rise.
- These top ~4–6 categories already contribute **50–60%** of total volume.
- By the time you include **Fishing, Water Sports, Camping & Hiking**, the cumulative curve approaches **~80%**.

This means the business is heavily driven by **sports gear, apparel, and outdoor activity products**, with long-tail categories contributing marginally.

---

##### Where most **destination activity** is concentrated (destination_country)

The top destination countries show an even stronger 80/20 effect:

- **United States, France, Mexico, Germany, Australia** dominate the first part of the curve.
- These top 5 countries alone contribute **~60–70%** of all shipments.
- Adding the next 5 (Brazil, UK, China, Italy, India) pushes the cumulative share to **~80–85%**.

This indicates that logistics operations are highly concentrated in a small set of major markets, with the remaining countries forming a long tail of low-volume destinations.

---

##### Where most **store presence** is concentrated (store_city)

Store distribution is extremely top‑heavy:

- **Caguas** alone contributes a disproportionately large share.
- Adding **Chicago, Los Angeles, Brooklyn, New York** quickly pushes the cumulative percentage above **70%**.
- The top ~10 cities account for **~85–90%** of total store count.

This suggests that physical presence is highly centralized, with a few cities driving the majority of store-based activity.

---

##### Interpretation of the 80/20 effect

Across all three dimensions:

- A **small number of categories** drive most product volume.
- A **small number of countries** dominate outbound logistics.
- A **small number of cities** hold the majority of store footprint.

This concentration implies:

- Inventory, forecasting, and replenishment efforts should prioritize the top categories.
- Logistics optimization should focus on the top destination countries.
- Store operations and resource allocation should

### 3. GEOGRAPHIC & MARKET VISUALISATIONS

```{python}
# Dynamic scatter map or country fallback
plot_geo_hexbin(df=fact_logistics_gold, lat_col="store_latitude", lon_col="store_longitude", gridsize=50)
plot_geo_hist2d(df=fact_logistics_gold, lat_col="store_latitude", lon_col="store_longitude", bins=50)
# plot_geo_kde(df=fact_logistics_gold, lat_col="store_latitude", lon_col="store_longitude")
plot_geo_hexbin_status(df=fact_logistics_gold, lat_col="store_latitude", lon_col="store_longitude", status_col="delivery_status", gridsize=50) 

```

##### Geographic concentration patterns

The store‑coordinate maps show that your shipment origins are not evenly distributed across the network. Instead, they form a **highly centralized geographic footprint**. Most store locations fall within a broad band between roughly –150 and –50 longitude and 20–50 latitude. This corridor represents the **core operational zone**, where the majority of shipments originate. Outside this region, store locations become sparse, indicating that the logistics network is built around a dominant central region rather than a wide national or global spread.

The hexbin density layer makes this clear: density increases by several orders of magnitude as you move into the central corridor, confirming that most shipment volume is generated by a relatively small set of store clusters.

---

##### Hotspots and high‑volume store clusters

Across the hexbin, KDE, and histogram views, the same hotspots consistently emerge:

- A **primary super‑cluster** of stores dominates the entire network.  
- This cluster sits within the mid‑latitude band and mid‑longitude band, forming the geographic “center of gravity” for your operations.
- The KDE map shows a smooth, continuous density field around this region, indicating that it is not just a single store but a **dense region of store locations**.
- The 2D histogram confirms that one or two grid cells contain disproportionately high counts, demonstrating a strong **80/20 effect** in store distribution.

This means your logistics network is effectively **hub‑centric**, with one major region driving the majority of outbound shipments.

---

##### Late‑delivery clusters relative to store locations

When delivery status is overlaid on the hexbin map, a clear pattern emerges:

- **Late deliveries cluster inside the same high‑density store region**.  
  This indicates that delays are not caused by remote or hard‑to‑reach stores but by **operational load within the core region**.
- On‑time and advance shipments appear more evenly distributed, suggesting that the underlying processes work well when volume is moderate.
- Cancellations and late deliveries appear more frequently at the **edges of the high‑density zone**, where store density begins to thin out. This often reflects:
  - capacity constraints,
  - routing inefficiencies,
  - or carrier performance issues in transitional zones.

The overlay shows that late deliveries are **volume‑driven**, not geography‑driven. The stores generating the most shipments are also the stores most likely to experience delays, which is typical in networks where throughput exceeds local processing or carrier capacity.

---

##### Combined interpretation

Taken together, the maps reveal a consistent geographic story:

- Shipment origins are **heavily concentrated** in one dominant region.
- This region forms the **operational core** of your logistics network.
- High‑volume store clusters create **hotspots** that drive most of the network’s activity.
- Late deliveries are not random; they **align with the same high‑volume clusters**, indicating that operational bottlenecks—not distance—are the primary cause.
- Improving performance in this central region would have the **largest impact** on overall delivery reliability.

A natural next step is to examine whether late deliveries correlate with specific stores, carriers, or time windows within this hotspot region, which would help isolate the root causes more precisely.

### 4. TEMPORAL PATTERNS & SEASONALITY

#### Order Volumn

```{python}
# Daily order volume, revenue, late deliveries, sessions
# if order_id unique per row, this approximates volume
plot_time_series(fact_logistics_gold, "order_date", "order_id", 
                 freq="D", title="Daily Order Volume (Proxy by order_id count)", y_label="Number of Orders")
```


##### Interpretation of Daily Order Volume (Demand Cycles, Spikes, and Drops)

The daily order volume shows a clear operational rhythm with identifiable demand cycles and several notable anomalies:

- **Stable baseline demand** persists from early 2015 through mid‑2017, with daily orders generally fluctuating within a predictable band. This indicates a mature, steady demand pattern with no major structural volatility during this period.

- **Recurring short‑term cycles** appear throughout the series. These cycles likely reflect:
  - weekly ordering behaviour (weekday vs weekend effects),
  - periodic promotional activity,
  - or natural customer demand rhythms.

- **Seasonal uplift patterns** are visible in several multi‑month stretches where order volume trends slightly upward before returning to baseline. These may correspond to seasonal shopping periods or business‑specific cycles.

- **A major structural drop** occurs abruptly around mid‑2017. Order volume falls sharply from its long‑standing baseline and stabilises at a much lower level. This is not a normal fluctuation and suggests:
  - a data pipeline or ingestion issue,
  - a change in business operations (e.g., store closures, system migration),
  - or a significant external event affecting demand.

- **No recovery after the drop** is visible through the end of the series. The sustained lower volume indicates that the shift is permanent rather than a temporary disruption.

Overall, the time series reflects a healthy, cyclical demand pattern for over two years, followed by a sudden and persistent decline that warrants investigation to determine whether it reflects a real operational change or a data quality issue.

#### Revenue

```{python}
plot_time_series(fact_logistics_gold, "order_date", "revenue", freq="ME", 
                 y_label="Revenue ($)", title="Monthly Revenue ($) — Seasonality and Peak Periods")
```

##### Interpretation of Monthly Revenue (Seasonality and Peak Periods)

The monthly revenue trend illustrates a highly consistent operational performance for the majority of the period, followed by a significant growth phase and a subsequent sharp contraction:

- **Stable revenue corridor** is maintained from January 2015 through the end of 2016. During this time, monthly revenue consistently oscillates between approximately $850k and  $950k, suggesting a well-established market presence with predictable cash flow.

- **Moderate seasonal fluctuations** are visible within each year. Minor dips often occur around the first quarter (notably in early 2015 and 2016), followed by steady recoveries. These fluctuations represent natural business cycles rather than structural instability.

- **A sustained growth surge** begins in early 2017. Revenue breaks out of its historical range, climbing steadily to reach a series peak of over $1M around September 2017. This indicates a successful expansion, a high-performing promotional period, or increased market demand during this specific year.

- **A critical and precipitous decline** occurs after the Q3 2017 peak. Revenue drops sharply throughout the final quarter of 2017, falling below $400k by January 2018. The velocity of this drop is anomalous compared to the historical stability of the previous years.

- **Potential Root Causes** for the late 2017 crash mirror the "major structural drop" seen in order volumes, likely pointing toward:
  - Incomplete data reporting for the most recent months.
  - A major strategic pivot or decommissioning of key revenue streams.
  - Significant external disruptions or loss of a major contract/channel.

Overall, the revenue data reflects nearly three years of healthy, steady performance with a notable growth peak in late 2017, though the terminal drop-off requires immediate investigation to distinguish between a reporting lag and a genuine business crisis.

#### Shipping Delays

```{python}
plot_time_series(fact_logistics_gold, "order_date", "shipping_delay", freq="W", 
                 title="Weekly Average Shipping Delay (Days)", 
                 y_label="Avg Shipping Delay (Days)")

plot_time_series(
    fact_logistics_gold,
    "order_date",
    "is_delayed",
    freq="W",
    y_label="Late Deliveries",
    title="Weekly Late Deliveries — Identifying Systematic Delay Periods"
)

```

##### Weekly Shipping Delays and Late Deliveries

The analysis of shipping performance reveals a sustained period of high operational friction followed by a sudden, drastic shift in the metrics:

- **Systematically high delay baseline** is evident from early 2015 through late 2017. During this multi-year stretch, the average shipping delay frequently spiked between **600 and 800 days**, with weekly late deliveries consistently hovering in the **600 to 700 range**. This suggests a long-term, systemic bottleneck in the fulfillment process rather than isolated incidents.

- **High volatility in delay duration** characterizes the first three years. While the *number* of late deliveries remained relatively stable, the *average delay time* shows aggressive weekly fluctuations. This indicates that while the backlog was constant, the severity of delays for specific orders was highly unpredictable.

- **A sharp structural "correction"** occurs in late 2017 (matching the timeline of the revenue and volume drops). Both the average shipping delay and the count of late deliveries plummet simultaneously:
  - Average delays dropped from ~800 days to a new baseline of **200–300 days**.
  - Late delivery counts dropped from ~700 to roughly **250**.

- **Correlation with declining volume** is the most likely driver of this "improvement." The reduction in delays is likely not due to improved efficiency, but rather a byproduct of the significant drop in total order volume seen in other metrics. With fewer orders to process, the system's relative strain decreased, though delays still persist at an objectively high level.

- **Terminal anomaly** is visible at the very end of the series (January 2018), where metrics dive again. This reinforces the suspicion of a data cutoff or a complete cessation of specific business operations.

Overall, the data identifies 2015 through Q3 2017 as a period of chronic operational underperformance. The subsequent improvement in delay metrics is statistically significant but likely a "false positive" resulting from the collapse of overall business activity during that same period.

#### Browsing Seasonality and Traffic Peaks

```{python}
plot_time_series(sessions_features_gold, "session_date", "page_views", freq="D",   
                 title="Daily Page Views — Identifying Daily Patterns and Anomalies", 
                 y_label="Page Views")

plot_time_series(sessions_features_gold, "session_date", "page_views", freq="W",   
                 title="Weekly Page Views — Identifying Weekly Patterns and Anomalies", 
                 y_label="Page Views")

plot_time_series(sessions_features_gold, "session_date", "page_views", freq="M",   
                 title="Monthly Page Views — Identifying Monthly Patterns and Anomalies", 
                 y_label="Page Views")
```

The analysis of page view data across daily, weekly, and monthly granularities reveals a highly stabilized traffic baseline interrupted by a singular, massive engagement anomaly:

- **An extreme traffic spike** is the defining feature of this period, occurring in mid-September. Daily page views surged from a baseline of ~3k to over **40,000**, while weekly views peaked near **60,000**. This suggests a high-impact event, such as:
  - A major viral marketing campaign or product launch.
  - A seasonal "flash sale" event (e.g., Anniversary Sale).
  - A technical anomaly or bot-driven traffic surge that was quickly mitigated.

- **Stable "Floor" of engagement** follows the September peak. From October through January 2018, traffic settles into a remarkably flat and consistent rhythm. Monthly views remain steady at approximately **80k–85k**, while daily views fluctuate within a tight band of **2k–3k**.

- **Absence of typical e-commerce seasonality** is notable during the Q4 period. Interestingly, there is no significant uplift in November or December, months typically associated with holiday shopping peaks. This suggests that the platform’s audience does not follow standard retail seasonal trends or that the "true" peak for this business occurs earlier in the year.

- **A terminal drop-off** is visible in the weekly view at the end of January 2018. Similar to the revenue and order volume metrics, the traffic falls sharply toward zero, reinforcing the hypothesis of a data reporting cutoff or a planned decommissioning of the platform.

- **High consistency in weekly patterns** is evident in the daily chart. Outside of the September spike, the small, recurring "sawtooth" pattern indicates a regular weekly cycle—likely driven by higher traffic during weekdays compared to weekends (or vice versa), which is typical for B2B or routine-based browsing behavior.

Overall, the browsing data reflects a "spike and plateau" narrative. The business successfully captured massive attention in September but failed to translate that into a long-term growth trend, instead returning to a modest but very stable baseline until the final data collapse.

#### Seasonal Decomposition of Revenue

```{python}
# Seasonal decomposition: logistics (multiplicative), sessions (additive)
plot_seasonal_decomposition(fact_logistics_gold, date_col="order_date", value_col="revenue", 
                            dataset_type="logistics",
                            title="Seasonal Decomposition of Revenue — Multiplicative Model with Monthly Seasonality")
```

The multiplicative decomposition provides a granular look at the underlying drivers of revenue, isolating the long-term trajectory from recurring patterns and random noise:
